title: Rust on White Cotton
timeEstimate: 30 minutes to several hours in sun; never use chlorine bleach
stainChemistry: Rust is iron(III) oxide (Fe₂O₃) — an INORGANIC metal stain, fundamentally different from organic stains. Iron oxide particles physically lodge in cotton fiber texture and bond through electrostatic interactions. The critical rule: chlorine bleach DARKENS rust by further oxidizing the iron — it converts soluble iron species to more-insoluble rust and sets the stain permanently. Only ACIDS and REDUCING agents remove rust. Acids (citric / ascorbic / oxalic) chelate the iron and dissolve it back into soluble form that rinses out. Reducing agents (sodium hydrosulfite professionally) convert rust back to soluble iron. Cotton is chemistry-tolerant so the protocol can be aggressive; the fiber isn't the worry, the chemistry choice is.
whyThisWorks: Lemon juice (citric acid) + salt + sunlight is the classic home rust remover — UV-assisted acid chelation. Works reliably on white cotton for light rust. Commercial rust removers (Whink, Iron Out, Bar Keepers Friend) use oxalic acid, which is stronger and works on heavier rust. Sodium hydrosulfite (pro-grade) is the reducing-agent alternative. The hard rule — NEVER chlorine bleach — is why "just throw it in the washer with bleach" (the reflex for white cotton) makes rust worse.
defaultAssumption: Rust on white cotton is commonly from a metal hanger, a wet iron, a clothesline, a metal zipper/snap, a washer drum, or tools. Small spots = easy lemon+salt+sun. Large area (wet cotton sat on a rusty surface) = commercial rust remover or pro. Old set-in rust that has been bleached = often permanent.

spottingProtocol.technique: Confirm rust and eliminate the cause
spottingProtocol.instruction: Confirm it's rust (orange-brown, often where cotton met metal). Find and remove the source — wet cotton on a wire hanger, a leaky iron, a rusty washer drum — otherwise it will happen again. If the source can't be removed, protect the cotton going forward.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Fresh lemon juice or bottled 100% lemon juice, table salt
spottingProtocol.instruction: Squeeze lemon juice directly on the rust spot. Sprinkle table salt generously over the wet lemon juice. Lay the cotton flat in direct sunlight (the UV accelerates the reaction). Let sit 15–30 min. Check — rust often fades visibly. Rinse cool, repeat if needed. Sun is not optional; it's the catalyst.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Whink Rust Stain Remover, Iron Out, or Bar Keepers Friend
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Per label, usually 30 seconds to a few minutes
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply per product label — most require dilution or very short contact. Oxalic acid is strong; rinse cool and thorough immediately. Gloves. Ventilate.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Oxalic acid is toxic if ingested and a skin/eye irritant. Gloves + ventilation. Never mix with bleach.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse cool until no trace of acid remains. Wash cool per care label. Acid left in cotton can weaken fibers over time. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
Sun-dry outside. UV + remaining moisture helps fade any residual rust shadow on white cotton. This is a classic technique and still works.

steps: 3. Oxalic acid (Whink / Iron Out) if lemon plateaus — gloves + ventilation.
products: Commercial rust remover (Whink / Iron Out / Bar Keepers Friend)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat can set iron oxide deeper
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach — darkens rust permanently
NEVER mix oxalic acid with chlorine bleach
Gloves + ventilation on commercial rust removers
Rinse acid thoroughly — residue weakens cotton fibers
Remove the rust source or it recurs

homeSolutions.instruction: This is the hard rule on rust: chlorine bleach DARKENS rust permanently by oxidizing the iron further. The instinct with white cotton is "throw it in with bleach," and it's exactly wrong here. If someone has already bleached the rust, the stain may now be permanent — take it to a pro. Otherwise, read on.

homeSolutions.agent: Find and Remove the Rust Source
homeSolutions.instruction: Figure out what rusted onto the cotton — a wire hanger, a wet iron, a rusty washer drum, a metal snap, tools in a laundry basket. Remove or clean the source, otherwise this will keep happening. Check wet-iron drip on ironing board cotton is the sneakiest one — scale inside the iron releases rust when you steam.

Squeeze fresh lemon juice (or bottled 100% lemon juice) directly on the rust spot until it's wet. Sprinkle regular table salt generously over the lemon. Lay the cotton FLAT IN DIRECT SUNLIGHT — not shade, not indoors — for 15 to 30 minutes. The citric acid dissolves the rust, the salt helps hold it in place, and UV sunlight catalyzes the reaction. Check the spot: on light / fresh rust you'll often see it visibly fade.

Rinse the spot thoroughly with COOL water. If rust remains, repeat lemon + salt + sun. Light rust often clears in one round; older or heavier rust may need 2–3 rounds. Cool water only — heat can set iron oxide deeper into the weave.

homeSolutions.agent: Commercial Rust Remover for Stubborn Spots
homeSolutions.instruction: If lemon + sun plateau, try a commercial rust remover — Whink Rust Stain Remover, Iron Out, or Bar Keepers Friend. These use oxalic acid (stronger than citric). READ THE LABEL — most require dilution and very short contact time. Work outside or in a well-ventilated room. Wear gloves.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Oxalic acid is toxic if swallowed, irritating to skin and eyes. Gloves + ventilation. NEVER mix with chlorine bleach — creates toxic gas.

homeSolutions.instruction: Wash on cool per care label to remove all acid residue. Line-dry outside in direct sunlight — UV + residual moisture can fade any remaining shadow. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. If rust is still visible, heat will set it deeper.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate — Pro for Set / Bleached / Heavy Rust
homeSolutions.instruction: If rust survives lemon + oxalic acid or someone already used chlorine bleach, take it to a pro dry cleaner. They have sodium hydrosulfite (a reducing agent) and related chemistry that can sometimes recover what home chemistry can't. Tell them: "Rust on white cotton, [old / fresh / source known or unknown], home-treated with lemon + salt + sun [+ Whink]. [No bleach / bleach was tried by accident]."

steps: 1. NEVER chlorine bleach — darkens rust permanently.
2. Find + remove the rust source (hanger, iron, washer drum).
3. Lemon juice + salt, lay flat in DIRECT SUNLIGHT 15–30 min.
4. Rinse cool, repeat lemon+salt+sun up to 3 rounds.
5. Commercial rust remover (Whink / Iron Out) for stubborn.
6. Cool wash, sun-dry line. Inspect BEFORE dryer.
7. Escalate if bleached or set — pro sodium hydrosulfite.
products: Whink Rust Stain Remover / Iron Out / Bar Keepers Friend
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if chlorine bleach has already been used (may be permanent), if rust survives oxalic acid, or if the rust source can't be identified and will recur.
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach on rust — makes it permanent
NEVER mix oxalic acid rust removers with bleach — toxic gas
Gloves + ventilation on commercial rust removers
Rinse acid thoroughly — residue weakens cotton over time
Cool water only — heat sets iron oxide
Remove the source or it recurs

neverDo: Never chlorine bleach on rust — it darkens permanently
Never mix oxalic acid products with chlorine bleach
Never skip the rinse after acid treatment
Never dryer heat on residual rust
fiberSensitivities: White cotton tolerates citric acid, oxalic acid, sodium hydrosulfite
Dyed cotton: oxalic acid can strip dye — hidden-seam test (use rust-cotton card for dyed)
Cotton-synthetic blends: usually tolerate acid treatment, test first

materialWarnings: NEVER chlorine bleach — the core rule
Remove the source or it recurs
Sun is part of the chemistry, not optional

escalation.when: Chlorine bleach has already been used on the rust, rust survives oxalic acid, rust source can't be found and will recur, or the rust has been through a hot dryer multiple times.
escalation.whatToTell: "Rust on white cotton, age [fresh / old / unknown], home-treated with lemon + salt + sun [+ Whink / Iron Out]. Source [known: X / unknown]. [No chlorine bleach used / bleach was accidentally used]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with sodium hydrosulfite / metal-stain chemistry

contextQuestions: Where did the rust come from — hanger, iron, washer, tools?
Has chlorine bleach been used on it already?
Has it been through a dryer?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The rust-source question is the most important — wet cotton on a wire hanger causes this repeatedly if not addressed. Iron scale (rust inside a steam iron) is a sneaky recurring source. Once bleach has touched rust, the stain is often permanent.
